title: What is PAYE? — Revenue
content:
PAYE (Pay As You Earn) is a method of deducting income tax and Universal Social Charge (USC) from your salary or wages. Your employer deducts these taxes from your pay before you receive it, and sends the deductions directly to Revenue.

How PAYE works
When you start a job in Ireland, your employer registers you for PAYE. Revenue issues a Revenue Payroll Notification (RPN) to your employer. The RPN tells your employer how much tax to deduct from your pay, based on your tax credits and rate bands.

Revenue Payroll Notification (RPN)
The RPN replaces the old P2C certificate. It is updated automatically when your tax credits or rate bands change — for example, if you claim a new tax credit or your circumstances change. Your employer downloads an updated RPN before each payroll run.

Tax credits and rate bands
Your tax credits reduce the amount of tax you pay. For example, if your tax liability is €5,000 and you have credits of €3,750, you only pay €1,250. Rate bands determine which income is taxed at 20% (standard rate) and which is taxed at 40% (higher rate).

End of year — Income Tax Return
Each year, Revenue calculates your final tax liability. If too much tax was deducted, you get a refund. If too little was deducted, you must pay the balance. You can view and manage your PAYE record using myAccount on the Revenue website.

myAccount
myAccount is Revenue's online self-service portal. You can use it to:
- View your tax credits
- Claim additional tax credits (e.g., medical expenses, rent tax credit)
- Request a review of your tax for the previous 4 years
- View your Revenue Payroll Notifications
- Register or deregister for PAYE

PAYE Exclusion Order
If you are a non-resident employee who works partly in Ireland and partly outside Ireland, your employer may apply for a PAYE Exclusion Order to avoid double taxation on your non-Irish income.

Emergency Tax
If your employer does not receive an RPN for you, they must apply emergency tax. Emergency tax rates are higher than normal — typically 40% — and you will pay more tax than necessary until an RPN is issued. You should register with Revenue immediately if you are on emergency tax. You can claim back overpaid emergency tax.

Useful contacts
For PAYE queries, contact Revenue's PAYE helpline or use the MyEnquiries service via myAccount.

title: Rent Tax Credit — Revenue
content:
The Rent Tax Credit is a tax credit available to renters in private-rented accommodation in Ireland. It was introduced in Budget 2023 and has been extended and increased in subsequent budgets.

Amount
The Rent Tax Credit for 2024 and 2025 is €1,000 per year for a single person, or €2,000 per year for a jointly assessed couple. You can claim it for the current year and for the previous four years if you were renting.

Who can claim it
You can claim the Rent Tax Credit if:
- You pay rent for your principal private residence in Ireland
- The property is not owned by a parent, child, or certain relatives
- The landlord is registered with the Residential Tenancies Board (RTB)
- You are not receiving any other housing support (e.g., Housing Assistance Payment)

How to claim
You claim the Rent Tax Credit through myAccount on the Revenue website:
1. Log in to myAccount
2. Select 'Review your tax' for the relevant year
3. Add the Rent Tax Credit under the 'Claimed credits' section
4. Enter your landlord's details and the amount of rent paid

Landlord registration requirement
Your landlord must be registered with the RTB. If they are not registered, you cannot claim the credit. You can check RTB registration at rtb.ie.

Student accommodation
Students renting from their college or university can also claim the Rent Tax Credit, provided the accommodation meets the qualifying conditions.

Claiming for previous years
You can make a retrospective claim for 2022, 2023, and 2024 if you were renting in those years and did not claim. The four-year limit applies — you can claim back to 2022 in 2025.

Important
The Rent Tax Credit reduces your tax liability. If your tax liability is zero (e.g., you earn below the exemption limit), you will not benefit from the credit as it is non-refundable.

title: Capital Gains Tax (CGT) — Revenue
content:
Capital Gains Tax (CGT) is a tax on the profit (gain) you make when you sell or dispose of an asset that has increased in value. It applies when you sell property (other than your main home), shares, cryptocurrency, business assets, or other capital assets.

CGT rate
The standard rate of CGT in Ireland is 33%. This applies to gains made on or after 5 December 2012.

Annual exemption
Every individual is entitled to an annual CGT exemption of €1,270. This means the first €1,270 of net gains in a tax year is exempt from CGT. Couples cannot combine their exemptions — each person has their own €1,270 exemption.

Principal Private Residence (PPR) relief
If you sell your main home (principal private residence), you are generally exempt from CGT. The property must have been your main home for the entire period of ownership. If you rented part of it or used it for business, partial CGT may apply.

How gains are calculated
Gain = Disposal price minus Acquisition cost minus Allowable expenses

Allowable expenses include:
- The original purchase price of the asset
- Legal and estate agent fees
- Stamp duty paid on acquisition
- Capital improvements made to the asset
- Indexation relief (available for assets acquired before 2003)

When to pay CGT
CGT is paid in two instalments:
- Gains made between 1 January and 30 November: pay by 15 December of the same year
- Gains made in December: pay by 31 January of the following year

You must file a CGT return (CG1 form) even if you have no tax to pay, if you disposed of a chargeable asset in that tax year.

Cryptocurrency
Revenue treats cryptocurrency as a capital asset. CGT applies to gains from selling, trading, or using crypto to buy goods or services. Each transaction is a disposal for CGT purposes. You must keep records of all cryptocurrency transactions.

Shares
Gains from selling shares are subject to CGT at 33%. The first €1,270 of net gains is exempt. Losses from share sales can be offset against gains in the same year or carried forward to future years.

Losses
Capital losses can be offset against capital gains in the same tax year. If losses exceed gains, the excess loss can be carried forward indefinitely to offset future gains.

title: Who Must File an Income Tax Return in Ireland — Revenue
content:
Most employees in Ireland pay tax through PAYE and are not required to file a tax return unless they have additional income or specific circumstances. However, you must file a Form 11 (self-assessment return) if you are self-assessed.

Who must file a tax return (Form 11)
You must file a Form 11 if you:
- Are self-employed (including sole traders and freelancers)
- Have rental income from property
- Have untaxed income above €5,000 per year (e.g., from foreign income, investments, or dividends)
- Are a proprietary director of a company
- Have income from a foreign pension
- Receive maintenance payments above a certain threshold
- Were asked by Revenue to complete a return

PAYE employees — Form 12
If you are a PAYE employee with minor additional income (under €5,000), you may be able to declare this on a Form 12 rather than a Form 11. Form 12 is simpler and available through myAccount.

Deadlines
- Paper return (Form 11): 31 October of the following year
- Online return (via ROS — Revenue Online Service): mid-November (extended deadline)
- Preliminary tax: must be paid by 31 October for the current year

Preliminary tax
If you are self-assessed, you must pay preliminary tax by 31 October each year. This is an estimate of your current year's tax liability. You must pay at least 90% of the final liability, or 100% of the previous year's liability, whichever is lower.

Revenue Online Service (ROS)
ROS is Revenue's portal for self-assessed taxpayers and businesses. You can file your tax return, pay tax, view your tax record, and manage VAT and payroll through ROS. Most self-assessed taxpayers are required to use ROS.

Surcharges for late filing
If you file your return late, Revenue will apply a surcharge:
- Up to 2 months late: 5% surcharge on the tax due (max €12,695)
- More than 2 months late: 10% surcharge on the tax due (max €63,485)

Penalties
Failure to file, fraudulent returns, or deliberate under-declaration can result in significant penalties and publication of your name on Revenue's defaulters list.

title: Flat Rate Expenses — Tax Relief for Workers — Revenue
content:
Flat Rate Expenses (FRE) are a type of tax relief available to employees in certain occupations. They cover the cost of tools, equipment, uniforms, and other expenses that employees must purchase for their job but cannot claim individually.

How they work
Flat Rate Expenses are agreed between Revenue and trade unions or representative bodies for specific occupations. Rather than requiring you to submit receipts, Revenue allows a fixed annual deduction based on your occupation. This reduces your taxable income and therefore your tax bill.

Who qualifies
Flat Rate Expenses are available to employees in a wide range of occupations, including:
- Healthcare workers (nurses, doctors, physiotherapists, etc.)
- Teachers
- Construction workers
- Shop assistants and retail workers
- Engineers
- Journalists
- Airline crew

The amount varies by occupation. For example, nurses can claim €733 per year, and teachers can claim €518 per year.

How to claim
You claim Flat Rate Expenses through myAccount on the Revenue website:
1. Log in to myAccount
2. Select 'Review your tax'
3. Add the relevant Flat Rate Expense for your occupation

Once claimed, it is applied automatically each year — you do not need to reclaim it annually.

Claiming for previous years
You can claim Flat Rate Expenses for the previous 4 years. If you have been working in a qualifying occupation and never claimed, you may be entitled to a refund.

Combining with other credits
Flat Rate Expenses work alongside other tax credits (PAYE credit, Personal Tax Credit, etc.) and further reduce your taxable income.

Medical Expenses Relief
Separate from Flat Rate Expenses, you can claim tax relief at 20% on medical expenses not covered by insurance. These include doctor visits, hospital stays, dental treatment (not routine), and prescribed medications. You must keep receipts and submit them via myAccount.
